audits.excerpt: Sylvanas knew anger in a body. Anger had heat, waste, movement. This was cleaner. He set his mouth and became all edge.
audits.reason: Intellectual taxonomy of emotion. The narrator catalogues anger as a concept ('heat, waste, movement') rather than showing what Sylvanas actually sees on Arthas's body in this moment. 'This was cleaner' is a label — cleaner HOW? What does his face do, what does his posture show, that reads as cleaner-than-anger to a ranger watching a man make a decision? The second sentence ('He set his mouth and became all edge') does physical work; the first three sentences are abstract scaffolding propping it up.
audits.suggestedFix: He set his mouth and became all edge. No heat. No wasted motion. His jaw went flat, his shoulders locked to the line of his spine, and the rest of his face just stopped.

audits.excerpt: Smaller without the magic. Harder hit.
audits.reason: Two emotional labels with no physical grounding. 'Smaller' — smaller how? Are her shoulders pulled in? Is the staff hanging instead of held? Is she shorter because she's leaning on the wall? 'Harder hit' is pure abstraction. A ranger-commander doesn't think in adjectives; she reads posture, breathing, weight distribution. Ground this in what Sylvanas can actually see.
audits.suggestedFix: Her shoulders had dropped. The staff hung loose in her grip instead of planted. She looked like she had been standing in that spot too long and the weight of it had started to compress her.

audits.excerpt: He put it under himself with the same cold control that had once looked like princely training and now looked like something meaner.
audits.reason: Retrospective narrator editorializing. 'Had once looked like princely training and now looked like something meaner' requires the narrator to compare across the arc of the story rather than staying in Sylvanas's present observation. When did she previously categorize his horse control as 'princely training'? This is arc analysis dressed as sensation. The mount sidestepping, the correction — that's concrete. The retrospective comparison is the narrator explaining the character's transformation to the reader.
audits.suggestedFix: He put it under himself without giving the animal a second thought. Economy, not grace. A man pared down to one task: put the city down before dusk could hand it to the dead.

audits.excerpt:
What she saw below was plain enough to turn her stomach.

Kill the living before the dead can claim them. Deny one great rising by breaking the city into manageable deaths.
audits.reason: The first sentence is a process-verb filter ('What she saw below was plain enough to...') that tells us about the act of seeing rather than showing the thing seen. The next two sentences are military-analysis exposition rendered as abstract thesis statements. Sylvanas has been concretely reading the city through districts, torch lines, and squad rotations for the last several paragraphs — she does not need to suddenly switch into essay mode to summarize her conclusion. The concrete details already carry the meaning. These sentences explain the horror to the reader instead of trusting the physical evidence that's already on the page.
audits.suggestedFix: Cut 'What she saw below was plain enough to turn her stomach.' entirely. Replace the two thesis sentences with one short grounded inference: 'Every team below was killing the next street before the last one could rise. District by district. Ahead of the dead instead of behind them.'

audits.excerpt: Only when they stopped did Sylvanas taste the hole they left in the city.
audits.reason: Forced synesthetic metaphor. You don't taste a hole. This tries to be literary and lands as personification of absence — the kind of metaphor that sounds clever but doesn't resolve into a physical sensation. The bells stopping is a powerful beat. Ground the absence in what actually fills the space once the bells are gone: the sounds that were always under them, now exposed.
audits.suggestedFix: Only when they stopped did the rest come through. Fire settling. Stone cracking where heat had found a joint. The wet drag of something heavy along a lane she could not see.

audits.excerpt: At that distance, Sylvanas could not make out the true color of his eyes, yet knew them wrong on sight. Fever-bright would have been easier. Wild would have been easier. This was emptied out.
audits.reason: Abstract emotional labeling. 'Fever-bright,' 'wild,' and 'emptied out' are psychological categories, not visual observations. At the stated distance Sylvanas cannot make out eye color — so what CAN she see? Posture. Gait. The tilt of the head. The speed or slowness of the turn. Ground the wrongness in what is visible at wall-to-gate distance rather than applying emotional adjectives to eyes she has already admitted she cannot see clearly.
audits.suggestedFix: At that distance Sylvanas could not read his eyes. She didn't need to. The angle of his head was wrong — tilted up, still, held for a beat too long. A man looking at something he had already decided about. No urgency left. No heat. Just the flat patience of a job finished.

audits.excerpt: She had the route by instinct.
audits.reason: Filter label. This tells the reader about the quality of Cyndia's navigation rather than showing it. The very next sentence ('Service lane, feed yard, wall stair, all from the grade underfoot and the warehouse backs built to shed carts') does the showing. The label is redundant scaffolding. Cut it and let the concrete detail carry the beat.
audits.suggestedFix: Cut the sentence. Let 'Service lane, feed yard, wall stair, all from the grade underfoot and the warehouse backs built to shed carts' open the paragraph directly.
